name: Solyndra
summary: Solyndra made cylindrical thin-film solar panels but collapsed after silicon prices dropped, undercutting its cost advantage, and it filed for bankruptcy just two years after receiving a federal loan guarantee.
lesson: A cost-structure advantage tied to a volatile input price (silicon) can vanish overnight and erase the entire business case.
source: New York Times, DOE Inspector General reports

name: SunEdison
summary: Once the world's largest renewable energy developer, SunEdison collapsed under a debt-fueled acquisition spree and filed for Chapter 11 in 2016.
lesson: Aggressive debt-financed roll-ups in capital-intensive industries can implode when growth outpaces cash flow discipline.
source: Reuters, Wall Street Journal bankruptcy coverage

name: A123 Systems
summary: MIT spinout A123 built lithium-iron-phosphate batteries for EVs and grid storage but filed for bankruptcy in 2012 after costly recalls and thin automotive margins; assets were sold to Wanxiang.
lesson: Being first to market with new battery chemistry doesn't protect against quality failures and margin pressure from automotive customers.

name: Better Place
summary: Better Place built battery-swapping infrastructure for electric cars in Israel and Denmark but shut down after burning through its funding with only a few thousand cars sold.
lesson: Building an entire proprietary infrastructure network before demand materializes is a capital trap few startups can survive.
source: The New York Times, Fast Company retrospectives

name: Beacon Power
summary: Beacon Power developed flywheel energy storage for grid frequency regulation and filed for bankruptcy in 2011, becoming the second DOE-backed energy company to fail that year after Solyndra.
lesson: Novel grid-storage technology needs a viable revenue market at commercial scale, not just technical feasibility, before heavy capital deployment.

name: Abound Solar
summary: Abound Solar made cadmium-telluride thin-film solar panels and shut down in 2012 after falling behind on efficiency and cost versus Chinese silicon panel makers.
lesson: Thin-film solar bets that lagged in efficiency were overwhelmed once Chinese silicon panel prices collapsed.

name: Konarka Technologies
summary: Konarka developed flexible organic photovoltaic solar film but shut down and liquidated in 2012 after failing to achieve commercially viable efficiency and cost at scale.
lesson: Breakthrough materials science demos don't guarantee a manufacturable, cost-competitive product.

name: Evergreen Solar
summary: Evergreen Solar pioneered a proprietary low-cost silicon wafer process but filed for bankruptcy in 2011, unable to compete with heavily subsidized Chinese manufacturers.
lesson: A domestic manufacturing cost edge can be wiped out by foreign competitors backed by state subsidies and massive scale.

name: Solar Trust of America
funding: Backed by Solar Millennium AG, conditional $2.1B DOE loan
summary: Solar Trust of America was developing the large Blythe solar thermal project in California but filed for bankruptcy in 2012 after its German parent Solar Millennium went insolvent.
lesson: A project developer's fate can be tied to a parent company's solvency even when the project itself remains technically sound.

name: Ener1
summary: Ener1 made lithium-ion batteries for EVs through subsidiary EnerDel and filed for Chapter 11 in 2012 after its main customer, Think Global, went bankrupt and demand fell short of projections.
lesson: Concentrating revenue on a single early-stage EV customer creates existential risk if that customer fails.

name: Range Fuels
funding: $320M incl. $80M DOE grant and $80M USDA loan guarantee
summary: Range Fuels aimed to convert wood waste into cellulosic ethanol and biofuels but never reached commercial-scale production, shutting its Georgia plant and defaulting on federal loans in 2011.
lesson: Scaling novel conversion technology from pilot to commercial plant is often far harder and costlier than projected.

name: Sungevity
summary: Residential solar installer Sungevity, known for satellite-based remote quoting, filed for bankruptcy in 2017 after overexpansion and executive turmoil drained its cash.
lesson: A clever customer-acquisition technology cannot offset unsustainable installation and financing cost structures.

name: SunPower Corporation
summary: Once a leading US solar panel and residential installer, SunPower filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in August 2024 after halting lease/PPA support and cutting a quarter of its workforce, selling its installation assets to Complete Solaria for $45 million.
lesson: Even a decades-old market leader can collapse when financing costs rise and dealer-channel unit economics turn negative.

name: Aquion Energy
summary: Aquion Energy developed non-toxic saltwater battery technology for grid storage, backed by Bill Gates, but filed for bankruptcy in 2017 after failing to scale manufacturing profitably.
lesson: Safer, more sustainable battery chemistry alone can't win if manufacturing costs stay uncompetitive with lithium-ion at scale.

name: STR Holdings
summary: STR Holdings made solar panel encapsulant materials and filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2016 after demand collapsed amid oversupply and price crashes across the solar supply chain.
lesson: Suppliers to a commoditizing industry get squeezed even harder than the manufacturers they serve during a price war.

name: Suntech Power
funding: Publicly traded (NYSE: STP), billions in debt
summary: Once the world's largest solar panel manufacturer, China's Suntech Power defaulted on $541M in convertible bonds and its main subsidiary entered bankruptcy in 2013 amid a global panel price collapse.
lesson: Being the largest manufacturer offers no protection when an entire industry oversupplies itself into a price collapse.

name: LDK Solar
funding: Publicly traded (NYSE: LDK), billions in debt
summary: Chinese silicon wafer maker LDK Solar filed for bankruptcy protection in 2014 after accumulating massive debt during the solar oversupply crisis of the early 2010s.
lesson: Debt-financed capacity expansion timed at the top of a commodity cycle leaves no room to survive the downturn.

name: Q-Cells SE
summary: Germany's Q-Cells was once the world's largest solar cell manufacturer by output but filed for insolvency in 2012 after Chinese competitors drove panel prices down faster than it could cut costs.
lesson: Manufacturing scale in solar cells didn't protect a European incumbent from lower-cost Asian competition.

name: Solon SE
summary: German solar module maker Solon SE filed for insolvency in 2012, one of several German solar manufacturers to collapse amid the industry-wide price war with Chinese producers.
lesson: National subsidy-driven demand doesn't guarantee domestic manufacturers survive global price competition.

name: Conergy AG
summary: German solar company Conergy filed for insolvency in 2013 after years of losses from the same Chinese-driven module price collapse that felled Q-Cells and Solon.
lesson: An entire national industry cluster can be wiped out together when a structural oversupply shock hits a commoditized product.

name: ClearEdge Power
summary: ClearEdge Power made stationary fuel cells for distributed power generation and filed for bankruptcy in 2014, with assets acquired by Doosan.
lesson: Distributed fuel cell generation needs a clear cost-per-kWh advantage over grid power to justify continued capital infusions.

name: Coda Automotive / Coda Energy
summary: Coda pivoted from selling an EV sedan to focusing on its lithium-ion battery and energy storage division, but the automotive arm filed for bankruptcy in 2013 after weak sales.
lesson: A late pivot to energy storage couldn't rescue a company after its core EV product failed commercially.

name: eSolar
summary: eSolar developed modular solar thermal tower technology but wound down operations around 2016 as utility-scale photovoltaic panels became far cheaper than solar thermal.
lesson: A technically elegant solar thermal architecture lost out once conventional photovoltaic costs fell faster than anyone predicted.

name: SolarReserve
funding: $700M+ project financing incl. $737M DOE loan guarantee (Crescent Dunes)
summary: SolarReserve built the Crescent Dunes molten-salt solar thermal plant in Nevada, but chronic technical failures and underperformance led to default on its power contract and bankruptcy of the project entity around 2019-2020.
lesson: Cutting-edge molten-salt storage at utility scale carried operational risks that undermined the entire project's bankability.

name: Sunverge Energy
summary: Sunverge made residential battery storage systems paired with solar and virtual power plant software but ceased operations in 2018 after running out of funding.
lesson: Being early to residential battery storage and virtual power plants wasn't enough before the market and utility incentives matured.

name: Envia Systems
summary: Envia Systems claimed breakthrough high-energy-density lithium-ion battery chemistry and signed a GM development deal, but the technology failed to meet cycle-life and safety targets, and the company was effectively wound down after litigation with GM.
lesson: Overstating lab-scale battery performance to a major automotive partner can trigger both technical failure and legal collapse.

name: Primus Power
summary: Primus Power developed zinc-bromide flow batteries for grid-scale storage but wound down operations around 2019 after failing to reach commercial scale profitably.
lesson: Flow battery chemistries promise long-duration storage but require enormous capital to reach cost parity with lithium-ion at scale.

name: ViZn Energy Systems
summary: ViZn Energy Systems made zinc-iron flow batteries for grid storage and ceased operations in 2018 after running out of funding to reach commercial scale.
lesson: Novel flow battery startups face a long, expensive road from pilot deployments to bankable commercial products.

name: Imergy Power Systems
summary: Imergy Power made vanadium redox flow batteries for microgrids and grid storage but shut down in 2016 after running out of capital.
lesson: Vanadium flow battery economics remained uncompetitive against falling lithium-ion prices during the mid-2010s.

name: Fluidic Energy (NantEnergy)
summary: Fluidic Energy (later rebranded NantEnergy) built zinc-air batteries for microgrids in emerging markets but wound down after failing to reach sustainable commercial scale despite heavy investment from Patrick Soon-Shiong.
lesson: Emerging-market microgrid battery deployments face slow sales cycles that can outlast even well-funded patient capital.

name: XL Fleet (Spruce Power predecessor)
summary: XL Fleet, a hybrid/electric fleet drivetrain and energy company that went public via SPAC in 2020, sold off its core business in 2023 and rebranded as Spruce Power after its stock collapsed amid weak commercial traction.
lesson: A SPAC listing brought capital and hype but couldn't substitute for a fleet-electrification product with real unit economics.

name: Romeo Power
summary: EV battery pack maker Romeo Power went public via SPAC in 2020 but, after mounting losses and inability to scale production, was acquired by Nikola in 2022 in what was effectively a distressed sale.
lesson: A SPAC's capital infusion cannot fix a battery manufacturer's inability to hit production and cost targets.

name: Hyzon Motors
summary: Hyzon Motors, a hydrogen fuel cell truck maker, went public via SPAC in 2021 but faced accounting restatements, executive departures, and collapsing sales, leaving the company a shell of its former valuation by 2025.
lesson: Hydrogen trucking startups need real fueling infrastructure and fleet demand, not just SPAC hype, to survive past the demo phase.

name: Proterra
summary: Electric bus and battery systems maker Proterra filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2023 after years of losses and supply chain constraints, later selling its bus and battery units separately.
lesson: Heavy-duty electrification requires enormous upfront capital that can outpace even well-funded public companies' ability to reach profitability.

name: Northvolt
summary: Swedish battery gigafactory Northvolt, Europe's most-funded battery startup, filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in November 2024 after trying to build six factories simultaneously while its flagship plant produced a fraction of its rated capacity, prompting BMW to cancel a $2B order.
lesson: Attempting to scale multiple gigafactories at once before proving reliable yield at even one plant multiplies execution risk beyond what capital can cover.

name: Britishvolt
funding: £200M+ raised, plus UK government support pledges
summary: UK battery gigafactory startup Britishvolt collapsed into administration in January 2023 after running out of cash before securing sufficient private investment to build its planned Northumberland plant.
lesson: Announcing a gigafactory before securing full construction financing leaves a battery startup exposed to any funding gap.

name: FREYR Battery (now T1 Energy)
summary: Norwegian-founded FREYR Battery cancelled its planned $2.6B Georgia battery gigafactory and rebranded as T1 Energy in 2025, abandoning its original battery cell manufacturing mission to pivot to solar module production.
lesson: When battery cell economics and demand don't materialize on schedule, even well-capitalized gigafactory plans can be abandoned entirely.

name: Sunnova Energy International
funding: Publicly traded (NYSE: NOVA), $8.9B in long-term debt at filing
summary: Residential solar financier Sunnova filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in June 2025 after rising interest rates, California policy changes, and a terminated DOE loan guarantee pushed it into insolvency; its assets were sold for about $118 million.
lesson: A solar-lease financing model built on cheap debt becomes fatally exposed when interest rates rise and government support is withdrawn.
source: Houston Public Media, CNBC, Kroll restructuring filings

name: Nikola Corporation
funding: Publicly traded (NASDAQ: NKLA), billions raised via SPAC
summary: Hydrogen and electric truck maker Nikola, whose founder Trevor Milton was convicted of fraud for faking a truck demo, filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in February 2025 and liquidated its assets to buyers including Lucid.
lesson: Fabricating product demonstrations to investors can eventually collapse a company even years after the fraud is exposed.

name: Griddy
summary: Texas retail electricity startup Griddy sold power at wholesale prices to consumers but collapsed in 2021 when Winter Storm Uri sent prices to the market cap, saddling customers with thousands of dollars in bills and triggering its shutdown by regulators.
lesson: Passing raw wholesale price volatility directly to consumers without a cap or hedge is a business model one extreme weather event away from disaster.

name: Just Energy Group
summary: Retail energy and natural gas marketer Just Energy filed for creditor protection in Canada and Chapter 15 in the US in 2021 after also being hit hard by Winter Storm Uri's extreme price spikes in Texas.
lesson: Retail energy resellers carry hidden tail risk from extreme wholesale price events that can wipe out years of accumulated margin.

name: Volta Industries
summary: EV charging network operator Volta Industries, known for ad-supported free charging stations, went public via SPAC in 2021 but was sold to Shell in 2023 for a fraction of its prior valuation after running low on cash.
lesson: An advertising-subsidized free-charging model didn't generate enough revenue per station to offset high hardware and site costs.

name: FreeWire Technologies
summary: FreeWire Technologies made battery-integrated EV fast chargers designed to avoid costly grid upgrades but underwent major layoffs in 2024 amid a broader EV charging slowdown and funding difficulties.
lesson: Clever engineering to sidestep grid infrastructure costs still needs matching EV adoption timing to sustain the business.

name: Lightning eMotors
summary: Commercial EV and charging infrastructure company Lightning eMotors went public via SPAC in 2021 but filed for bankruptcy in 2024 after failing to scale production profitably in a slower-than-expected commercial EV market.
lesson: Small-scale commercial EV converters face brutal capital requirements that a SPAC raise alone can't guarantee will be sufficient.

name: KiOR
funding: $600M+ incl. public listing and state loans
summary: KiOR aimed to convert wood biomass directly into renewable crude oil using a proprietary catalytic process but filed for bankruptcy in 2014 after its Mississippi plant never reached promised yields.
lesson: Promising catalytic biomass-to-fuel conversion at commercial yields is far harder to prove than pilot-scale results suggest.

name: AltaRock Energy
summary: AltaRock Energy pursued enhanced geothermal systems using engineered fracturing to access deep heat, but its flagship Newberry Volcano project was abandoned after inducing seismic concerns, and the company wound down operations.
lesson: Enhanced geothermal fracturing techniques carry induced-seismicity risks that can halt a project regardless of the underlying resource potential.

name: Amyris
summary: Synthetic biology company Amyris originally aimed to produce renewable diesel and jet fuel from engineered yeast but pivoted repeatedly toward consumer products before filing for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2023.
lesson: Repeated strategic pivots away from a founding energy mission can signal an unresolved core unit-economics problem rather than solve it.

name: Enron Corporation
summary: Energy trading giant Enron, once a pioneer of deregulated energy markets, collapsed into bankruptcy in December 2001 after systemic accounting fraud using off-balance-sheet special purpose entities was exposed.
lesson: Innovative energy trading models built on hidden debt and fraudulent accounting eventually collapse regardless of market position.
source: SEC enforcement records, Wall Street Journal

name: Bloom Energy
summary: Bloom Energy makes solid-oxide fuel cell systems for distributed on-site power generation and completed a successful IPO in 2018, continuing to grow revenue as a public company serving data centers and enterprises.
lesson: Patient capital and a decade of iteration on fuel cell reliability can eventually produce a durable public company.
source: SEC S-1 filing, company financial reports

name: SolarEdge Technologies
summary: SolarEdge developed power optimizer and inverter technology for solar installations, went public in 2015, and became a global leader in solar power electronics before facing a cyclical downturn.
lesson: A differentiated hardware layer (power optimizers) inside a commoditizing industry (solar panels) can command lasting margin.

name: Enphase Energy
summary: Enphase pioneered microinverter technology for solar panels, went public in 2012, and grew into one of the most valuable solar technology companies by market capitalization.
lesson: Owning the electronics layer of solar systems, not just panel manufacturing, proved to be the more defensible and profitable position.

name: Sunrun
summary: Sunrun pioneered the residential solar lease/power-purchase-agreement model, went public in 2015, and became the largest residential solar installer in the US, later acquiring Vivint Solar in 2020.
lesson: Removing the upfront cost barrier through leasing unlocked mass-market residential solar adoption.

name: Vivint Solar
funding: Publicly traded, acquired by Sunrun in 2020 for ~$3.2B
summary: Vivint Solar built a large residential solar installation business and was acquired by rival Sunrun in a 2020 all-stock merger, giving its investors a strong consolidation exit.
lesson: Consolidation in a scale-driven industry can turn a standalone installer into a valuable acquisition target rather than a failure.

name: First Solar
summary: First Solar manufactures cadmium-telluride thin-film solar panels and has remained one of the few profitable, thriving vertically integrated US solar manufacturers through multiple industry price cycles.
lesson: Vertical integration and a proprietary lower-cost thin-film process let a manufacturer survive price wars that killed most peers.

name: Canadian Solar
summary: Canadian Solar grew from a solar module manufacturer into a diversified global solar and battery storage company with project development and manufacturing operations spanning many countries.
lesson: Diversifying across manufacturing, project development, and storage cushioned a solar company against single-segment price crashes.

name: JinkoSolar
summary: JinkoSolar grew to become one of the world's largest solar module manufacturers by shipment volume, surviving multiple industry oversupply cycles that killed many Chinese competitors.
lesson: Scale and cost discipline allowed one manufacturer to consolidate market share while less efficient peers went bankrupt in the same price war.

name: ChargePoint
funding: Publicly traded via SPAC (NYSE: CHPT) since 2021, $1B+ raised
summary: ChargePoint built one of the largest networked EV charging platforms in North America and Europe and, despite a volatile stock price since its 2021 SPAC listing, remains an operating market leader in commercial and workplace charging.
lesson: Being early to build a large networked charging footprint created switching costs and scale that newer entrants still struggle to match.

name: EVgo
funding: Publicly traded via SPAC (NASDAQ: EVGO) since 2021
summary: EVgo operates one of the largest public fast-charging networks in the US and has continued expanding through partnerships with automakers like GM despite the broader EV charging sector's growing pains.
lesson: Automaker partnerships for co-funded fast-charging buildouts provided a more durable growth path than advertising- or subscription-only models.

name: Blink Charging
summary: Blink Charging manufactures and operates EV charging equipment across residential, commercial, and DC fast-charging segments and has remained a going concern through the EV charging industry's shakeout of weaker rivals.
lesson: A diversified charging hardware and network business model provided more resilience than single-segment competitors during the sector's 2023-2024 downturn.

name: Beam Global
summary: Beam Global makes solar-powered, off-grid EV charging and energy storage infrastructure (EV ARC) and has maintained government and commercial contracts as a small but profitable-focused public company.
lesson: A niche off-grid charging product avoided direct commoditized competition with larger grid-tied charging networks.

name: QuantumScape
funding: Publicly traded via SPAC (NYSE: QS) since 2020, $1B+ raised
summary: QuantumScape is developing solid-state lithium-metal battery technology backed by Volkswagen and remains well-funded and operating, having begun shipping sample cells to automotive partners for qualification.
lesson: Deep-pocketed strategic automotive backing can sustain a pre-revenue deep-tech battery company through a much longer R&D runway than typical startups get.

name: Solid Power
funding: Publicly traded via SPAC (NASDAQ: SLDP) since 2021
summary: Solid Power develops solid-state battery cells and electrolyte materials with automotive partners including BMW and Ford and continues operating as a public company advancing its pilot production line.
lesson: Licensing electrolyte and cell technology to multiple automakers spreads risk versus betting on a single customer relationship.

name: Form Energy
summary: Form Energy is developing iron-air battery technology for multi-day grid energy storage and has raised substantial funding while building its first commercial manufacturing plant in West Virginia.
lesson: Targeting a distinct storage niche (multi-day duration) rather than competing head-on with lithium-ion attracted patient, large-scale capital.

name: Redwood Materials
summary: Founded by former Tesla CTO JB Straubel, Redwood Materials recycles lithium-ion batteries and produces battery materials, growing into a major supplier backed by partnerships with Panasonic, Ford, and Toyota.
lesson: Solving the battery supply chain's recycling and materials bottleneck attracted major automaker partnerships rather than competing directly on cell manufacturing.

name: Sila Nanotechnologies
summary: Sila Nanotechnologies develops silicon-based anode materials to boost lithium-ion battery energy density and has shipped its first commercial products in partnership with Mercedes-Benz and Whoop.
lesson: Focusing on a single high-value battery material component, rather than full cell manufacturing, reduced capital intensity while preserving upside.

name: Group14 Technologies
summary: Group14 Technologies produces silicon-carbon battery anode materials and has built commercial-scale manufacturing plants in Washington State and South Korea with backing from Porsche and SK Materials.
lesson: Strategic investment from automotive and materials incumbents de-risked the capital-intensive scale-up of a novel anode material.

name: Enovix
funding: Publicly traded via SPAC (NASDAQ: ENVX) since 2021
summary: Enovix makes advanced silicon-anode lithium-ion batteries with a novel 3D cell architecture, initially targeting consumer electronics, and continues ramping commercial production as a public company.
lesson: Targeting a smaller, higher-margin consumer electronics battery market first provided an easier path to initial commercial revenue than jumping straight to EVs.

name: Fluence Energy
funding: Publicly traded (NASDAQ: FLNC) since 2021, joint venture of Siemens and AES
summary: Fluence Energy, formed as a joint venture between Siemens and AES, became one of the world's largest grid-scale battery energy storage system integrators and completed a successful 2021 IPO.
lesson: Combining two established parent companies' balance sheets and industrial expertise let a storage integrator scale faster than a typical venture-backed startup.

name: Nel Hydrogen (Nel ASA)
summary: Norwegian company Nel ASA manufactures hydrogen electrolyzers and fueling stations and remains one of the largest publicly listed pure-play hydrogen technology companies, supplying industrial and mobility customers globally.
lesson: A century-old industrial gas heritage gave a hydrogen electrolyzer maker manufacturing credibility that newer entrants lacked.
source: Company investor reports, Oslo Stock Exchange filings

name: ITM Power
summary: UK-based ITM Power manufactures PEM electrolyzers for green hydrogen production and has continued operating and winning large-scale industrial hydrogen project contracts across Europe.
lesson: Early specialization in PEM electrolysis technology positioned the company for the green hydrogen policy tailwinds of the 2020s.
source: Company investor reports, London Stock Exchange filings

name: Plug Power
summary: Plug Power supplies hydrogen fuel cell systems for material handling and, more recently, green hydrogen production, and despite persistent losses has continued raising capital and operating as a major public hydrogen player for over two decades.
lesson: Long-running access to public capital markets can keep an unprofitable but strategically positioned hydrogen company alive far longer than a private venture-backed peer.

name: Hydrogenics Corporation
funding: Publicly traded, acquired by Cummins in 2019 for ~$290M
summary: Hydrogenics developed hydrogen fuel cell and electrolyzer systems and was acquired by engine maker Cummins in 2019, giving its shareholders a strong strategic exit as Cummins built out its hydrogen business.
lesson: Being acquired by a large industrial incumbent entering hydrogen validated the technology and delivered investors a successful exit.

name: Ormat Technologies
summary: Ormat Technologies designs, builds, and operates geothermal and recovered-energy power plants worldwide and has remained consistently profitable as one of the largest pure-play geothermal energy companies globally.
lesson: Owning and operating power plants directly, not just supplying equipment, gave a geothermal company durable recurring cash flow.

name: Fervo Energy
summary: Fervo Energy applies horizontal drilling and fiber-optic sensing techniques from oil and gas to enhanced geothermal systems, and has signed major power purchase agreements with Google and utilities while scaling its Nevada and Utah projects.
lesson: Adapting proven oil-and-gas drilling techniques to geothermal de-risked the core technology enough to attract big-tech offtake commitments.

name: Dandelion Energy
funding: $100M+ venture capital, spun out of Alphabet's X
summary: Dandelion Energy, spun out of Alphabet's X moonshot lab, installs residential geothermal heat pump systems and has continued to expand its home installation business across the northeastern US.
lesson: Spinning out of a well-resourced corporate lab gave a geothermal heating startup a technical and capital head start over garage-stage competitors.

name: Advanced Microgrid Solutions
funding: $50M+ venture capital, acquired by Fluence in 2019
summary: Advanced Microgrid Solutions developed software to aggregate and dispatch behind-the-meter battery storage for utilities and was acquired by Fluence Energy in 2019, folding its technology into a larger storage platform.
lesson: Software that orchestrates distributed battery assets became more valuable inside a larger integrator's platform than as a standalone company.

name: AutoGrid Systems
funding: $130M+ venture capital, acquired by Schneider Electric-backed Uplight in 2021
summary: AutoGrid Systems built AI-driven demand response and distributed energy resource management software for utilities and was acquired in 2021, merging into Uplight's grid software platform.
lesson: Grid software companies that prove utility-scale reliability can find strong exits through consolidation rather than needing to IPO independently.

name: Opus One Solutions
funding: $20M+ venture capital, acquired by GE in 2020
summary: Opus One Solutions developed grid management software for distributed energy resources and was acquired by General Electric in 2020 to strengthen GE's grid software offerings.
lesson: A specialized grid analytics platform became a strategic acquisition target for a major industrial player expanding into software-defined grid management.

name: Green Mountain Energy
funding: Acquired by NRG Energy in 2010 for ~$350M
summary: Green Mountain Energy was an early pioneer of retail renewable energy plans for consumers and was acquired by NRG Energy in 2010, continuing to operate as a green power brand under NRG.
lesson: Being an early mover in consumer green-power retail built a brand valuable enough for a large incumbent utility to acquire outright.

name: EVBox
funding: Acquired by Engie in 2017, later merged and continued operations under new ownership
summary: Dutch EV charging hardware maker EVBox was acquired by French utility Engie in 2017 for a reported nine-figure sum, giving early investors a strong exit while the company continued expanding across Europe and North America.
lesson: A utility parent's balance sheet and distribution channels helped a European charging hardware maker scale faster than as an independent venture.

name: Powin Energy
summary: Powin Energy became one of the largest global suppliers of grid-scale lithium-ion battery storage systems, scaling deployments across the US, Europe, and Asia while remaining privately held and profitable-focused.
lesson: Focusing purely on being a reliable large-scale battery storage integrator, rather than chasing multiple business lines, built durable market share.

name: Innowatts
summary: Innowatts provides AI-driven energy analytics and meter data management software for utilities and continued growing its customer base among major North American utilities.
lesson: Selling analytics software to slow-moving utility customers required patience but built a defensible recurring-revenue business.

name: Nuvve Corporation
funding: Publicly traded via SPAC (NASDAQ: NVVE) since 2021
summary: Nuvve develops vehicle-to-grid (V2G) technology that lets EV batteries feed power back to the grid, and continues operating and deploying pilot and commercial V2G projects with fleet and utility partners globally.
lesson: A vehicle-to-grid technology pioneer stayed alive by focusing on fleet and school-bus pilot deployments while the broader V2G market slowly matured.

name: 24M Technologies
summary: 24M Technologies, an MIT battery spinout, developed a semi-solid lithium-ion manufacturing process that simplifies cell production, and has licensed its technology to multiple global battery manufacturers including Kyocera and Fuji.
lesson: Licensing a novel, cost-reducing manufacturing process to established battery makers avoided the capital burden of building factories directly.
